intitule: Recognising Dialogue Structure and Politeness Formulas

enonce.texte:
Read the following short dialogue and identify the elements of a well-structured conversation.

Dialogue:

Mary: Good morning, sir. Could you please help me find the history section?

Librarian: Good morning, young lady. Of course. It's on the second floor, to your left.

Mary: Thank you very much. You've been very helpful.

Librarian: You're welcome. Have a nice day!

questions.texte: Identify the polite expressions used in the dialogue.
questions.reponse: 'Good morning', 'Could you please', 'Thank you very much', 'You're welcome', 'Have a nice day'

sousQuestions.texte: List all the polite expressions you can find.
sousQuestions.reponse: Good morning; Could you please; Thank you very much; You're welcome; Have a nice day

questions.texte: Is this dialogue formal or informal? Justify your answer.
questions.reponse: Formal (use of 'sir', 'young lady', 'Could you please', complete sentences)

sousQuestions.reponse: The characters use titles ('sir'), full polite forms, and complete sentences, which indicate a formal register.

intitule: Creating a Dialogue for a Given Situation

enonce.texte:
Write a dialogue of about 10 exchanges between two people in the following situation.

Situation: A student (David) is asking his teacher (Mrs. Atanga) for advice on how to improve his English marks before the end-of-term exam.

questions.texte: Create a dialogue respecting dialogue conventions (speaker names, punctuation, politeness).
questions.reponse: Example: David: Good morning, Mrs. Atanga. May I have a moment of your time? Mrs. Atanga: Good morning, David. Of course. How can I help you? David: I'm really worried about my English marks. Could you give me some advice on how to improve before the exam? Mrs. Atanga: Certainly. First, you should read more English texts and practice writing every day. David: That's a good idea. What about grammar? Mrs. Atanga: I recommend you review the tenses and conditionals. You can also do online exercises. David: Thank you very much, ma'am. I will follow your advice. Mrs. Atanga: You're welcome, David. Work hard and I'm sure you'll do better. David: I will. Have a nice day, ma'am. Mrs. Atanga: You too, David.

sousQuestions.texte: Write a dialogue of at least 8 exchanges.
sousQuestions.reponse: See response above for a complete example.

sousQuestions.texte: Include at least three polite expressions.
sousQuestions.reponse: Good morning; May I have a moment; Thank you very much; Have a nice day

intitule: Analysing a Dialogue for Context and Vocabulary

enonce.texte:
Read the following dialogue and answer the questions below.

Dialogue: At the Market

Customer: Hello. How much is this bag of rice?

Trader: Good afternoon. That one is 15,000 francs.

Customer: That's quite expensive. Can you give me a discount?

Trader: I can reduce it to 14,000 francs. That's my best price.

Customer: Alright, I'll take it. Here is the money.

Trader: Thank you. Here's your receipt.

Customer: Thank you. Have a good day.

questions.texte: Analyse the vocabulary related to purchasing goods in this dialogue.

sousQuestions.texte: List at least four words or phrases related to buying and selling.
What does the customer do to try to pay less?
sousQuestions.reponse: The customer asks for a discount.

questions.texte: Is the dialogue formal or informal? Justify.
questions.reponse: Semi-formal / Informal (uses 'Hello', 'Alright', but also polite forms like 'Thank you' and 'Good afternoon')

sousQuestions.reponse: The register is semi-formal: they use greetings like 'Good afternoon' and 'Thank you', but also casual expressions like 'Alright' and 'That's my best price'.

enonce.texte:
Read the poorly written dialogue below, identify its weaknesses, and rewrite it correctly.

Original Dialogue:

John: hey u there

P: yes

John: wut time is class

P: 8

John: ok thx

questions.texte: Evaluate the original dialogue. List its weaknesses.
questions.reponse: Weaknesses: use of abbreviations ('u', 'wut', 'thx'), lack of punctuation, no capitalization, missing politeness, no complete sentences, unclear context.

sousQuestions.reponse: 1. Abbreviations ('u', 'wut', 'thx'); 2. No capitalization or punctuation; 3. No politeness or complete sentences.

questions.reponse: Example: John: Hey, are you there? Peter: Yes, I'm here. John: Could you tell me what time the class starts? Peter: It starts at 8 o'clock. John: OK. Thank you very much. Peter: You're welcome.

sousQuestions.texte: Write a corrected version of the dialogue.
sousQuestions.reponse: John: Hey, are you there? Peter: Yes, I'm here. John: Could you tell me what time the class starts? Peter: It starts at 8 o'clock. John: OK. Thank you very much. Peter: You're welcome.

intitule: Creating a Dialogue for a Health Awareness Campaign

enonce.texte: Write a dialogue of about 12 to 15 exchanges between two students discussing the dangers of drug abuse and how to avoid peer pressure. Use appropriate vocabulary from the health and safety lessons.

questions.texte: Create a complete dialogue with a clear introduction, development, and conclusion.
questions.reponse: Example: Awa: Hi, Ambe. How are you doing? Ambe: I'm fine, Awa. But I'm worried about some of our classmates. Awa: Why? What's the problem? Ambe: I heard that some students are using drugs. They say it helps them relax. Awa: That's terrible! Drugs are very dangerous. They can cause addiction and serious health problems like heart disease and cancer. Ambe: Exactly. And I think some of them feel pressured to try drugs because others are doing it. Awa: Peer pressure can be strong, but we must say 'no'. We should tell them about the dangers. Ambe: Maybe we can start an awareness campaign at school. Awa: That's a great idea! We can invite a doctor to talk to the students. Ambe: And we can also create posters about the consequences of drug abuse. Awa: I think we should also encourage them to join sports and other healthy activities. Ambe: Yes, that way they can stay busy and make good friends. Awa: Let's talk to the principal about our campaign tomorrow. Ambe: Good idea. Together we can make a difference.

sousQuestions.texte: Use at least five vocabulary words from health or safety (addiction, heart disease, cancer, peer pressure, awareness campaign, etc.).
sousQuestions.reponse: Drugs, addiction, heart disease, cancer, peer pressure, awareness campaign, consequences, healthy activities, principal, make a difference.

sousQuestions.texte: Ensure correct punctuation and dialogue formatting.
sousQuestions.reponse: Each speaker is identified, dialogue is clearly structured, punctuation is correct.

intitule: Applying Dialogue Writing to a Real-Life Situation

enonce.texte: Write a dialogue between a young person and a community leader discussing solutions to teenage pregnancy in their community. The dialogue should include problems, causes, and proposed solutions.

questions.texte: Compose a well-structured dialogue of at least 14 exchanges.
questions.reponse: Example: Youth: Good morning, Chief. Thank you for meeting me. Chief: Good morning, my child. How can I help you? Youth: I'm worried about the high rate of teenage pregnancy in our village. Chief: Yes, it's a big problem. Many young girls drop out of school. Youth: I think one cause is lack of information about reproductive health. Chief: That's true. Parents are often shy to talk about it. Youth: Also, some girls are pressured by older men who promise them money. Chief: That's a serious issue. We need to talk to parents and educate them. Youth: Maybe we can have community meetings to discuss the problem. Chief: That's a good idea. We could invite health workers to give talks. Youth: We also need to support girls to stay in school. Chief: Yes. Education is the key. We can also create a scholarship fund for girls. Youth: That would be wonderful. What about boys? Should we educate them too? Chief: Of course. Boys need to learn responsibility and respect for girls. Youth: I think we should also involve the churches and mosques. Chief: Excellent suggestion. They can help spread the message. Youth: Thank you, Chief, for listening. I hope we can make a change. Chief: Thank you for your concern, my child. Together we can help our community.

sousQuestions.texte: Write a dialogue of at least 14 exchanges.
Include at least three causes and three solutions to teenage pregnancy.
sousQuestions.reponse: Causes: lack of information, pressure from older men, lack of parental communication. Solutions: community meetings, health talks, scholarship funds, involving religious leaders.

sousQuestions.texte: Use appropriate politeness formulas and vocabulary.
sousQuestions.reponse: Politeness: 'Good morning', 'Thank you', 'Thank you for listening', 'Thank you for your concern'. Vocabulary: teenage pregnancy, reproductive health, dropout, community, education, responsibility, scholarship fund, religious leaders.
